title: The Geopolitics of Chagos, Afghanistan's Apartheid, and the Gulf
content:
In Afghanistan, the Taliban regime has recently attempted to convince the international community that it has modernized its governance. Eager for foreign investment, the government invites travel influencers and promotes sports teams to shed its brutal reputation. However, these superficial public relations efforts cannot hide the horrific reality of the regime's systemic gender apartheid. Girls and women are strictly banned from attending schools, participating in employment, and engaging in public life. The regime absurdly believes it can build a functioning, modern economy while entirely excluding half of its population.

The situation for women recently worsened when the regime issued an extraordinarily draconian new penal code. This legislation severely restricts women's basic freedoms and imposes harsh punishments for minor infractions of their strict social rules. Even highly conservative nations have recognized the impossibility of such an oppressive system and begun dismantling gender apartheid. International observers consistently note that economic revival is utterly impossible without basic human rights and female workforce participation. Consequently, the regime's oppressive policies ensure that the country remains deeply isolated from the global diplomatic and economic community.
